Memorand 
To 
From 
Michael Bennett, Executive 
Fair Political Practices C 
1100 K Street 
Sacramento, CA 
95814 
Robert C. Summers 
:~ 
. ~~ctor 
ission 
Date 
: September 12, 1977 
File No.: M-l 
Subject: Political Reform Audit Division Audit Seminar 
May, 1977 
Our Political Reform Audit Division Audit Seminar Report brought 
forth some audit problem areas, questions and suggested solutions, 
three of which are attached. 
I hope they will prove of interest 
to you as feedback from our audit staff. 
Chief, Political Reform Audit Division 
Attachments 
cc: 
Doug Dick 
Dan Converse 
Jim Turrini 
Jim Stevens 


REC'O J 
1 1977 S.?ranchise Tax Board 
. Memorandum 
Seminar CorrillUttee 
Date 
: June 7, 1977 
File No.: ELM PR: JR: cn 
From 
JerF.f Richard 
Subject: 
.Hass l-failings 
Audit Problems: 
1. Inadequate co~~ttee records in this area when other phases of 
recordkeeping are good. 
2. People involved in campaign can't remember details concerning 
disposition of printed matter a~d use of postal account. 
3. Description of items on printer's invoices provide little de-
tail. Copies of items are usually not kept by the committee. 
4. It is difficult to reconcile printing bills vuth the postal 
account. 
A. 
Large printing orders are used at various tL~es throughout 
the campaign rather than being mailed in a single batch. 
B. 
Bulk rate postage is by piece rather than by '·leight. 
A 
single mailing often contains items from two or three 
printers folded together. 
C. 
The use of stamps, meters or mailing services compounds 
the audit problems. 
Audit Procedures: 
1. 
As a preliminary step oake a workpaper using a photocopy of 
list of items received by Comrr~ssion. 
Add iPJormation from 
the copies of the individual items in the audit package: 
an additional description of the item and name of printer. 
2. 
Obtain transcript of postal account. 
Tie do\iU at least the 
large mailings. 
3. Tie printer's invoices to the items listed on prelimina..ry .. :ork-
papers. 
Obtain copie s of items from large orders vJhich can t t 
be reconciled. 


Seminar Commit 
June 7, 1977 
Page 2 
Suggested Solution: 
1. 
RecorQ~eeping regulations for printed matter and postal ex-
pense should be established by the Commission. 
A. 
Copies of all printed items would be retained by the 
com.1U t tee. 
B. 
The met.hod of distribution of all printed items would be 
recorded. 
C. 
Items sent to the Commission i·;ould include a cover letter 
indicating the date mailed, quantity sent and the method 
of mailing. 
~Bk'kd 
Auditor, Southern District 
Political Reform Audit Division

State of !:aliforr.ia 
Franchise Tax Board 
Memorandum 
'0 
From 
Subject: 
PRAD Se~inar Co~~ittee 
Dote 
Hay 24, 1977 
File No.: JF: nk 
John Fraser 
PRAD Auditor Conference \,lorkshops 
The follovring is a sumnary of hlO v;orkshop discussions held on Hay 19 alld 20 
during the PRAD auditor conference at.Aerojet. 
Discussion centered on 
problems auditors had encollilltered during (ca~paign) audits of mass mailings 
and audit techniques useful in coping \.;i th those problems. 
As a result of 
the discussions, a conclusion alld recommendation .... las reached by consensus 
of the group members. 
Problems Encountered 
1. 
Reluct&llce of postal service employees to provide information 
regarding a bulk aCCOllillt. 
2. 
Committees do not keep useful records of mailings/c&llnot 
identify specific mailings to specific costs and printings. 
3. 
Audit time required to reconcile postal expenses to print-
ings and to contact third parties is usually not justified 
by the resultallt findings. 
4. 
Committee's use of stamps makes tracing mailings very 
difficult. 
5. 
Even when all printing costs and mailing costs are known, 
it is difficult, if not impossible, to identify specific 
mailing costs with copies of prLlltings. 
6. 
Copies of mass mailings did not reach the San Fr&llcisco 
office until after some Committee audits were cOr;Jpleted. 
7. 
FPPC advice to filers as to what types of mailings \vere 
required to be sent to that body '.-las inconsist&llt. 
For 
example, one co~~ittee is told by the FPPC that a thank 
you letter must be sent to the COr;Jmission; another 
committee is told a fundraiser invitation needn't be 
sent. 
Useful Audit Tec~~iaues 
1. 
Contacting third parties - postal service, committee volun-
teers, printers, ma~ling houses, etc. 
2. 
Revievl FPPC mass mailing index. 


PR~~ Seminar Commi 
May 24, 1977 
Page 2 
3u 
Utilizing experience of peers when possible. 
4. 
Review confirmation letters for nop~onetary contributions 
(printing, postage). 
5. 
Review FPPC opinions regarding mass mailings. 
6. 
Determine existence of mimeograph use by committees (when 
postal expense exists without correlating printing cost). 
Conclusion 
Tracing printings to mailings is a time consuming procedure which may 
not be justified by resultant findings, although all auditors agreed that 
auditor judgement should dictate the extensivenss of this part of a 
ca~paign 
audit. 
Recommendation 
Some minimal recordkeeping requirements for committees are needed and, if 
not required by regulation, should at least be provided as Recordkeeping 
Guidelines. 
Information desirable includes a copy of each mailing made, 
date a~d quantity sent, cost, method of postage used. 
(Auditors agreed 
that issua~ce of new re6Jlations to this effect is unlikely since other 
recordkeeping requireu;ents are vague and not specific. 
G~~~ 
~U~ 
Audit Group 03 


State of 'Colifomia 
Franchise Tax Board 
Memorandum 
From 
Subject : 
Conference Committee 
Dote 
J.1ay 24, 1977 
Mark Ford, ~tichele Holm, Jerry Richard 
File No.: 
CJT:mc 
Carmella J. Tamayo 
Fu.n.d Raisers 
Planning a fund raiser will usually correlate with the size of a campaign. 
"nether the fu.rld raiser be on a small or large scale, there will ahlays be 
a "plan of attac]t". 
This plan will usually start with invitations and also 
include a special attraction, entertai~~ent and refreshments. 
From the 
beginning to the end of the fund raiser, steps will be followed. 
Auditing 
these fund raisers requires knowledge of the planning steps involved and 
an insight of problems that might materialize. 
During the audit, the pro-
blems encountered center on internal control. 
Only through regulations or 
guidelines for campaign fund raisers can an auditor be assured that certain 
steps were taken to guarantee control over a fund raising event. 
It is the consensus of the group that in most cases internal control is 
weak in relation to a fund raising event. 
There is usually no control over 
the contributions received prior to or during the fund raiser. 
Nor is there 
control over the number of people in attendance and in relation to the amount 
of contributions received. 
In the area of nonmonetary contributions, there 
is usually no record kept. It is difficult for an auditor to determine possi-
bilities of nonmonetary contributions without any indication as to the type 
of fund raiser given. 
In regard to expenditures incurred, it is difficult to relate these expenses 
with the fund raising event. It is to the auditor's advantage if a relation-
ship between the expenses and the event can be established. 
This relationship 
would give the auditor an. insight to nonmonetary contributions and expenses 
paid by another party. 
In the case where another cOmmittee, whether it is a subcommittee or a se-
parate entity, gives a f~~d raiSing event on behalf of a candidate there may 
or may not be any control over such event by the candidatets controlled commi-
ttee. In this instance, a separate bank account could be established which 
would permit nondisclosure of contributions and expenditures. 
These problems 
fund raisers. 
quired to keep 
can be minimized through regulations or guidelines for campaign 
As part of a recordkeeping guideline, 
commi ttees should be re-
a record of each fund raiser. 
In addition, the date, 
and 


Mark Ford 
Mi chele Holm 
Jerr.r Richard 
Page 2 
May 24, 1977 
committee members involved with the fund raiser should be recorded. 
This requirement would aid the auditor in third party contacts and give 
her/him an idea o~ the scope and depth o~ the event. 
Although expenditures and contributions over $50 are already disclosed, 
there should be an indication either on the report or in the records i~ 
the expenses or the co~tributions relate to a ~und raiser. 
Nonmonetary 
contributions or expenses paid by a third party could be easily detected 
i~ correspondence among expenses, contributions and ~und raisers can be 
identi~ied. 
Control throughout the ~~~d raiser is essential. 
To insure control it 
should be present in the ~irst stages of planning. 
There should be control 
over the number o~ invitations sent or tickets sold prior to or during the 
event. 
Also a correlation should be attained betw'een the number of people 
who contributed with either the response cards ~rom invitations or number 
o~ tickets sold. 
In cases where there are "cash barrels" at the door, 
someone should be responsible ~or loggir~ in the amOQ~t received per person. 
This would curtail a.."'1Y cash contributions o~ $50 or more. 
In the cases where separate entitites sponsor a fund raiser for a candidate, 
they too, should be subject to the internal control requirements. 
In addi-
tion, the separate entity should secure authorization from the ca..~didate 
or her/his controlled committee to give the event. 
A record o~ the event 
should be kept by the controlled committee. 
In no way would the auditors want too stringent requirements that would 
eventually inhibit the campaign process. 
To meet this end our means must 
be reasonable and sound. 
Initially, there should be an Q~derstanding that 
these requirements are for the ca~aign's protection and that the steps 
taken to insure control would only expedite the audit. 
Ct:'V~ fl 
Auditor 
Coastal-Audit Group 01

sections.question_synthetic: This document is not a direct legal question but rather internal audit division memoranda discussing audit problems, procedures, and recommendations related to campaign finance reporting requirements for mass mailings and fund raisers.
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: modern, old; LLM: Document is a collection of internal audit division memoranda rather than an FPPC advice letter response to an external request. No traditional QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections present. Multiple memos from Franchise Tax Board staff describing audit challenges, procedures, and policy recommendations. Some OCR quality issues with character recognition in older document portions.

CONCLUSION: Tracing printings to mailings is a time consuming procedure which may 
not be justified by resultant findings, although all auditors agreed that

ca~paign 
audit. 
Recommendation

(Auditors agreed

embedding.summary: A series of internal FPPC Political Reform Audit Division memoranda from May-June 1977 documenting audit problems encountered during campaign audits, particularly regarding mass mailings and fund raisers, along with recommended audit procedures and suggestions for improved recordkeeping regulations and guidelines.
